nativeRange.habitat: Shallow rocky shorelines, tide pools, and reef margins down to about 10 m, often sheltering in empty shells, rubble, rock crevices, and dense algal turf.

diet.notes: In the wild, juveniles tend to feed on small invertebrates while adults shift toward a more herbivorous diet dominated by benthic algae. In captivity, generally accepts a varied diet of mysis and brine shrimp, finely chopped marine meats, and herbivore preparations or dried marine seaweed offered two to three times daily; readily takes prepared dry foods when tank-bred.

avoidWith: other Molly Miller Blennies in undersized tanks
delicate ornamental shrimp (may be picked at)

summary: A hardy, mottled tan-and-black combtooth blenny found across the Atlantic and Mediterranean, valued in reef aquariums for its appetite for nuisance algae and its tendency to pick at Aiptasia. Generally considered peaceful and beginner-friendly, it is periodically available as captive-bred stock from specialty marine retailers.
careNotes: The Molly Miller Blenny is commonly considered beginner-friendly and is generally regarded as reef-safe. It tends to be peaceful with dissimilar tankmates but can behave territorially toward other blennies or fish of similar body shape, so it does best as the only representative of its kind in a tank. A minimum footprint of around 120 liters is recommended, with plenty of live rock, rubble piles, and crevices to satisfy its perch-and-dart lifestyle and give it secure hiding spots. Adults are reported to reach around 10–12 cm in total length. Like other blennies, the species has been reported as a capable jumper, so a tightly fitting lid is generally advised. It is widely regarded as a useful algae grazer, and individuals have been reported to pick at nuisance Aiptasia anemones — though this behavior varies by specimen and is not guaranteed. Reef-safety is generally considered good, but individuals may occasionally nip at small ornamental shrimp. Lifespan in well-kept aquaria is commonly reported in the range of 7–9 years.
breedingNotes: The Molly Miller Blenny is oviparous, spawning in pairs on cleaned substrate. Eggs are demersal and adhesive, anchored via a filamentous pad, and the male guards the clutch until hatching. Captive breeding has been established commercially, and tank-bred individuals are periodically available in the hobby, but rearing the pelagic larvae is generally considered moderately difficult for home aquarists.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Husbandry baseline: 30 US gal minimum, 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12, peaceful (aggressive only toward similarly shaped fish), reef compatible, omnivore, max 4 in, moderate care, Caribbean origin.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy authority: Blenniidae, Blenniiformes, Salariinae. Max 12.0 cm TL, depth 0-10 m, tropical, marine reef-associated. Distribution: Western Atlantic (Bermuda, Florida, Gulf of Mexico to Brazil), Eastern Atlantic (Mauritania, Canary Islands to South Africa), Mediterranean, Northwest Pacific (Japan, Taiwan). Juveniles feed on invertebrates, adults shift to algae. Oviparous with demersal adhesive eggs.

additional.name: Sustainable Aquatics
additional.notes: Tank-bred source. Confirms 4 in adult size, >30 gal recommended, peaceful temperament, Caribbean origin. Tank-bred individuals are weaned onto captive dry foods (Hatchery Diet), supporting 'easy' feedingDifficulty.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive-bred via paired spawning on cleaned substrate; males guard demersal adhesive eggs. Larval rearing is pelagic and challenging for hobbyists, though commercial captive-bred stock is regularly available.
